Derwent & Shotley BridgeDerwent & Shotley BridgeStreet Gate, near East Law; about midway between Shotley Bridge and Ebchester. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating until after 1830.toggle('NZ05E_detail') toggle('NZ05E_info', 'NZ05E_detail') this.style.cursor='help'On the newer Gateshead to Shotley Bridge turnpike road through Litzford and Hamsterley (1833-1888), now the A694, loosely following the line of the Derwent. Toll house demolished in the 1930s.FugarNZ25BSheep Hill, Burnopfield; in fork to catch Wolsingham and Shotley Bridge Traffic (now A6310). Very closeto the Whickham township boundary. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.On the turnpike from Gateshead to Shotley Bridge (1793-1874) where it branches South towards Wolsingham. Now the A692. Now the B6310. this.style.cursor='help'Farnley BarJust West of junction with A6321 & East of Devil’s Water, it appears to be on the Gateshead & Hexham turnpike but the toll house was erected by Hexham Turnpike Trust in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.toggle('NY96C_detail')
toggle('NY96C_info', 'NY96C_detail')
this.style.cursor='help'On the Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (A695). Earliest mention in 1820. Different positions indicated by Greenwood and OS. Click for more detail >>.toggle('NY96C_info', 'NY96C_detail')
this.style.cursor='help'DilstonNY96CNY96CAt the Cross Roads (NE corner), adjacent to the ‘Big Club’. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.toggle('NZ16B_detail')
toggle('NZ16B_info', 'NZ16B_detail')
this.style.cursor='help'On the Gatehead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871) which turned southwards from here before Crawcrook’s ‘New Road’ was built in the 1930’s. Now the B6317, previously the A695. Click for more detail >>.CrawcrookNZ16BNear the Board Inn (now Miji restaurant) at Stella. Probably somewhere between Stella Lane (leading to Crookhill), and the traffic lights at Haugh Lane (leading to Newburn Bridge). Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.toggle('NZ16C_detail')
toggle('NZ16C_info', 'NZ16C_detail')
this.style.cursor='help'On the B6317 (once the A695). The Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871) formerly took a more southerly route through Path Head and Crookhill (Hexham Old Road). Click for more detail >>.toggle('NZ16C_info', 'NZ16C_detail')
this.style.cursor='help'StellaNZ16COld Bridge End Cottage; Sands Lane (The Pavilion), Swalwell; may have gone but promising structure by bridge. Toll house built in 1779 by Gateshead & Hexham Turnpike Trust. Operating until after 1830toggle('NZ16D_detail')
toggle('NZ16D_info', 'NZ16D_detail')
this.style.cursor='help'Toll bar on the East side of Swalwell Bridge on the Gatehead to Hexham turnpike. Once the A695 now the B6317. Earliest evidence: 1833. Toll house still stands. Branch End
At the turn-off for New Ridley, just on the Mickley township boundary. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830. Virtually on the township boundary.toggle('NZ16J_detail')

toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail')On the A6321. A continuation of the Hexham turnpike coming from the West and continuing East to Heddon-on-the-Wall. The turnpike operated from 1751 to 1876. toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail')On the Hexham turnpike (B6530) leading East from here to join the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike, and West into Hexham and onwards to Haltwhistle. toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail')On the B6528 (once the A69) a stretch of the Hexham turnpike (1751-1876), which links Hexham to Heddon-on-the-Wall. Click for more detail >>.UnnamedNZ06IBluebellHoughton, by Blue Bell Inn. Probably at the head of Bluebell Lane opposite Houghton Moor Cottages. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating until after 1830.North side of Scotswood Bridge (the old Chain Bridge) just East of the modern Bridge. Toll house built in 1831. Operating until after 1830.However, it's not close to any particular bridge in Newcastle and even further from Scotswood Bridge. Further investigation is required here.toggle('NZ16J_detail') toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail') this.style.cursor='help'SCOTSWOOD BRIDGE On the A695, the Scotswood Bridge Toll. Earliest evidence: 1833. toggle('NZ16J_detail') toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail') this.style.cursor='help'ELSWICK Claimed to be a toll point for the Scotswood Road approch to an unnammed bridge. Earliest evidence: 1838. NZ26HNZ26TNZ16HOld Redheugh BridgeScotswood BridgeWylam BridgeThe original toll house built in 1836 by a bridge trust for the collection of tolls for on the south side of the bridge and demolished 1960.toggle('NZ16J_detail') toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail') this.style.cursor='help'WYLAM BRIDGE The new toll house for Wylam Bridge was built in 1899 by a bridge trust on the North side of bridge and still stands.Old toll house; at the south end of Redheugh Bridge. Alongside the A608. Toll house built in 1871 by a bridge trust. Operating until after 1830.toggle('NZ16J_detail') toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail') this.style.cursor='help'OLD REDHEUGH BRIDGE Toll bar for old Redheugh Bridge, just West of the modern bridge. Now an unclassified road. Toll house still stands.Tolls, initially of one penny, were charged until abolition of tolls in 1937. Bridge designed by Robert Stephenson and opened on 15 August 1849.toggle('NZ16J_detail') toggle('NZ16J_info', 'NZ16J_detail') this.style.cursor='help'HIGH LEVEL BRIDGE On the North side of the Bridge, between the Castle Keep and the Bridge Hotel. All On/OffPhase 1: 1746 - 1751 Phase 2: 1771 - 1778Phase 3: 1792 - 1796Phase 4: 1810 - 1815Phase 5: 1826 - 1833Non-Turnpike Tolls

© Land of Oak & Iron Trust 2023AcombXOn the Hexham to Alnmouth turnpike which operated from 1751, now the A6079. On Cross Bank/Front Street, between Acomb & Wall. Situated North of the previous position shown on the Greenwood map of 1820 (NY96I). Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830. XIn medieval times public roads were barely more than muddy tracks. Responsibility for maintaining them depended on the customs of the manor.
During the Tudor period, Highways Acts were passed to improve the condition of major public roads and liability for their maintenance fell on the parish, supervised by Justices of the Peace. Parishioners were required to provide free labour on four days of the year, later extended to six. Land owners had to provide horses and carts. The Justices were later given powers to raise money by local taxation.
This system proved to be inadequate. During the industrial revolution, the Government assigned sections of major roads to individuals or companies with the right to extract tolls from road users. These were the Turnpike roads. Their proprietors were the Turnpike Trusts.
This map shows the development of the Turnpikes in the area. The heavy lines show the Turnpikes on Greenwood's map of 1820. The fainter lines show the side roads on that map. The green diamonds show toll bars on Greenwood's map. The red diamonds are the toll bars shown on the 1st Edition Ordnance Survey maps of the 1850s.Brunton (West)XOn the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881), now the B6318. On the Military Road at Brunton Bank on Greenwood maps (1820) but West of later position (NY96F). Evidence found only on the Greenwood map on Northumberland (1827).
Brunton (East)XOn the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881) now the B6318. Toll house still stands. On Military Road, Brunton Gate; Brunton Bank; close to roadside; East of Greenwood location (NY97-H). Right on the Cockslaw township boundary. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating until after 1830.
UnnamedXOn the A6079, the Alston Turnpike, continued North of Hexham which operated from 1751. At the cross-roads (Hawford Lane), near Acomb, South of later site (NY96A). Evidence found only on the Greenwood map on Northumberland (1827).
Branch End (Shildon)XOn the Military Road, the B6318, Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881). It might have guarded the entrance to the Hexham turnpike for Corbridge (now the B6321), but the Grid reference has it just East of turn-off. Toll house built in the 19th century by Hexham Turnpike Trust. Operating in 1830.
Harlow Hill (West)XOn the B6318 which was part of the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881). On the eastern edge of Harlow village Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Harlow Hill (East)XOn the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881). Now the B6318, the old A69. At Rudchester, just West of Stamfordham Road. On old boundary. Building may still exist. Toll house built in the 19th century. Not operating in 1830.
ThrockleyXOn the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881). Previously the A69 but now the B6528. West of Throckley village at junction with Drove Road and Hill House Road; also listed as Benwell. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
DentonXOn the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881), the A186, once the A69. At the roundabout where the West Road becomes the A69 as it crosses the current A1. Denton Hall. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Throckley (Benwell)XOn the A186, once the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881). The two toll bars close together here are probably the same one at different dates. Benwell Hills; also listed as Benwell. On the West Road at the head of Colston Sreet, (just East of Condercum Road.) Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Fox & Hounds (West)XOn the West Road (A186), the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike (1750-1881). Just off Benwell Bank. At the foot of Two Ball Lonnen. Toll house seems to be a little way up Two Ball Lonnen, built in the 19th century. Not operating in 1830.
Kenton & SideXOn the Newcastle upon Tyne to the River Wansbeck turnpike (which then goes on to Wooler under a different trust). Today the A167. Earliest evidence: 1824. Just before it becomes the A696; on the Ponteland Road at the junction with Kenton Lane & Etal Lane. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
FenhamXOn the Newcastle upon Tyne to the River Wansbeck turnpike (which then goes on to Wooler under a different trust). Today the A167. Where the turnpike running West along Ponteland Road meets Stamfordham Road and turns North. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
BenwellXOn the A186, once the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike. The two toll bars close together here are probably the same one at different dates. On the West Road between Ladykirk Road & Hamptstead Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Gosforth & SideXCow Causey and Buckton Burn turnpike (1746-1875) from Newcastle towards Alnwick and Berwick. The Great North Road, B1318, formerly the A1. On Gosforth High Street just North of the junction with Salters Road & Church Road. On the border with South Gosforth township. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
WalkerXOn the A193, the North Shields to Newcastle turnpike. On Shields Road, by the junction with Benfield Road & Coutts Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Byker HillXOn the North Shields to Newcastle turnpike, now the A193. Earliest evidence: 1824. On Sheilds Road, at Burton Street, at the top of Byker Bank. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Tyne BridgeXOn the Durham to Tyne Bridge turnpike (Great North Road), this was the toll point for the old Tyne Bridge. Just off the A167 (old A1) to the West. On the edge of the river on the South side of the old Tyne Bridge (about half way between the present-day Tyne and Swing Bridges). Toll house built in 1772. Operating in 1830.
GatesheadXA branch off the Durham to Tyne Bridge turnpike (1746-1871), an easterly continuation of the Gateshead to Hexham turnpike, the A184 today. Windmill Hill. Probably on Park Lane, within a mile of the Great North Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Shipcote (Old)XOn the older Durham to Tyne Bridge turnpike (1746-1871), now the B1296. Old Durham Road; gate on the older branch where Shipcote Lane continues West as Split Crow Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Not operating in 1830.
Shipcote XOn the newer section of the Durham to Tyne Bridge turnpike (1746-1871). The Great North Road, now the A167. On Durham Road (new in 1824) where it crosses Shipcote Lane. West of older gate on Old Durham Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Birtley LaneXOn the Durham to Tyne Bridge turnpike (1746-1871). The Great North Road, now the A167). Birtley; on Durham Road at the junction with Lamesley Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Blue BellXOn the B6528 (once the A69) this was the Eastern end of the Hexham Turnpike (1751-1876) about a mile from where it joins the Newcastle to Carlisle road. East of the Horsley toll bar and probably later in date. Houghton, by Blue Bell Inn. Probably at the head of Bluebell Lane opposite Houghton Moor Cottages. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830. For more informations see (http://heddonhistory.weebly.com/blog/the-blue-bell)
Horsley (Hollyn Hall)XOn the B6528 (once the A69), at the Eastern end of the Hexham Turnpike (1751-1876) slightly West of the Bluebell turnpike which it probably pre-dated. Horsley; at the cross-roads with the road north from Wylam. Opposite Holleyn Hall entrance. Toll house built in the 19th century. Not operating in 1830.
UnnamedXOn the B6528 (once the A69) a stretch of the Hexham turnpike (1751-1876), which links Hexham to Heddon-on-the-Wall. On the old A69, just West of Horsley at the turn-off for Harlow Hill (N). Evidence found only on the Greenwood map on Northumberland (1827).
StyfordXOn the Hexham turnpike (B6530) leading East from here to join the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike, and West into Hexham and onwards to Haltwhistle. The turnpike operated from 1751 to 1871. Toll house still stands. Toll Bar Cottages, No. 72. Toll house built in the 19th century.
St Helen'sXOn the A6321. A continuation of the Hexham turnpike coming from the West and continuing East to Heddon-on-the-Wall. The turnpike operated from 1751 to 1876. On Aydon Road at the foot of St. Hellen’s Lane. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
BoroughXOn the A1426, the Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871) before the Lobley Hill turnpike branches off. Bensham Road, just metres South West of the junction with Coatsworth Road. Near the bus stop. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Low TeamsXOn the A1114, which was the Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871). Approximately at the end of Ropery Road where it meets Derwentwater Road. Adjecent to the A184. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Derwent BridgeXToll bar on the East side of Swalwell Bridge on the Gatehead to Hexham turnpike. Once the A695 now the B6317. Earliest evidence: 1833. Toll house still stands. Old Bridge End Cottage; Sands Lane (The Pavilion), Swalwell; may have gone but promising structure by bridge. Toll house built in 1779 by Gateshead & Hexham Turnpike Trust. Operating until after 1830
Swalwell BankXOn Whickham Bank (B6317). A branch off the Newcastle to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871). On Whickham Bank, West of Whickham. Slightly North (downhill) of the entrance to North View.
StellaXOn the B6317 (once the A695). The Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871) formerly took a more southerly route through Path Head and Crookhill (Hexham Old Road). Near the Board Inn (now Miji restaurant) at Stella. Probably somewhere between Stella Lane (leading to Crookhill), and the traffic lights at Haugh Lane (leading to Newburn Bridge). Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Branch EndCrawcrookXOn the Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871) which turned southwards from here before Crawcrook’s ‘New Road’ was built in the 1930’s. Now the B6317, previously the A695. At the Cross Roads (NE corner), adjacent to the ‘Big Club’. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Branch EndXOn the A695, Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871) where a branch heads South through New Ridley to join the West Auckland turnpike. At the turn-off for New Ridley, just on the Mickley township boundary. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830. Virtually on the township boundary.
UnnamedXOn the A695, Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1778-1872). Between the railway bridge and the station. Close to where the road branches north for Bywell bridge. Evidence found only on the Greenwood map of Northumberland (1827).
New Ridley BarXOn a branch of the Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1777-1871) heading South through New Ridley to join the West Auckland turnpike at Scales Cross. At New Ridley, on the New Ridley Road. West of the car park, East of Lead Lane. Operating in 1830.
Farnley BarXOn the A695, Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (1750-1881). Toll house still stands. Farnley Gate House; to the West of Riding Mill. Close to the Riding township boundary. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating until after 1830.
DilstonXOn the Gateshead to Hexham turnpike (A695). Earliest mention in 1820. Just West of junction with A6321 & East of Devil’s Water, it appears to be on the Gateshead & Hexham turnpike but the toll house was erected by Hexham Turnpike Trust in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Hexham Low (Fairshaw)XOn the B6305, the Hexham to Alston branch of the Alston turnpike. Earliest evidence: 1802. On the South East side of the bend at Lowgate. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Westboat BridgeXHexham to Alnmouth, heading North from Hexham on the West side of the North Tyne, at Warden Bridge. Toll house still stands. Toll House at Bridge End, on southern bank by the bridge over river. Toll house built in 1826 by a bridge trust. Operating until after 1830.
FellingXOn the B1426 which was the Gateshead to Sunderland turnpike. Earliest evidence: 1820. On Sunderland Road, Felling. Near to Reginald Street. Slightly west of the spot suggested by Greenwood, which would place it in Heworth township. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Lingey LaneXOn the Gateshead to Sunderland turnpike, now the A184. On the Felling by-pass at the foot of Lingey Lane. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
FugarXOn the turnpike from Gateshead to Shotley Bridge (1793-1874), the A692 on modern maps. Toll house demolished in the 1930s. Street Gate; On Lobley Hill Road about midway between Lobley Hill and Sunniside. Just East of Little Dean Cottage. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
CrookXOn the turnpike from Gateshead to Shotley Bridge (1793-1874) where it branches South towards Wolsingham. Now the A692. Toll house demolished in 1910. Sheep Hill, Burnopfield; in fork to catch Wolsingham and Shotley Bridge Traffic (now A6310). Very closeto the Whickham township boundary. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Shieldrow Lane EndXOn part of the Lobley Hill turnpike that branches North from Wolsingham towards Gateshead (1793-1874). At a bend on Durham Road, junction with Shield Row Lane. On the Kyo/West Moor township boundary. Operating in 1830.
LongcloseXOn the old Gateshead to Shotley Bridge turnpike (1793-1874), now the B6309. Toll house still stands. West of Hamsterley Mill just off A694 which didn’t exist at the time of Greenwood Maps; Longclose Bank, old junction with Tollgate Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating until after 1830.
Snows GreenXOn the old Gateshead to Shotley Bridge turnpike which originally took the high road through Burnopfield and Medomsley (1793-1874). Now the B6310. At the foot of Benfieldside Road, just off Snow’s Green Road. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
AllensfordXOn the Aukland to Otterburn turnpike (now the A68), which joins the Hexham Turnpike and turns North at Corbridge and operated from 1792. South of bridge but North of Pemberton Road. Appears to be just South of the county/township boundary, West of Consett. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Kiln Pit HillXOn the A68, Auckland to Otterburn turnpike (from 1793), which joins the Hexham Turnpike and turns North at Corbridge. Appears to be at the Kiln Pit Hill crossroads where Grey Mare Hill and Barley Hill Lane meet the A68. Not operating in 1830.
WhitesideXOn an unclassified road that was once part of the A68, the West Auckland turnpike (from 1792), catching traffic heading South from the Gateshead/Hexham road. Near Low Farm, Broomhaugh, on the older route of the A68 which was diverted to join the A695 East of the village. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
Stagshaw BankXOn the Auckland to Otterburn turnpike (A68) which operated from 1792. Where the A68 crosses the Military Road (B6318), the Newcastle to Carlisle turnpike. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
LeadgateXOn the Durham to Shotley Bridge turnpike (from 1810). On Durham Road/Watling Street, at junction with St. Ives Road/Front Street. A692 now diverted to the South. Operating in 1830.
Berry EdgeXNo known turnpike here. Appears to be a branch of the Durham to Shotley Bridge turnpike. Barhouse Lane, Berry Edge (lane to Consett Iron Works). Now a road linking Delves Lane to Medomsley Road. Operating until after 1830.
Shotley Bridge Low Gate (Slonks)XOn the A694, the Durham to Shotley Bridge turnpike. Just North of Shotley Grove Road. Named Slonks in 1831 Act; Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
ConsettXNo known turnpike here. May be to be a branch of the Durham to Shotley Bridge turnpike. Old Lane, Berry Edge (lane absorbed into Consett Iron Works, now west of Genesis Way, North of Tesco’s car park.) Operating until after 1830.
Shotley BridgeXOn the turnpike from Shotley Bridge to Edmondbyers (1815-1873). The B6278 on modern maps. Toll house still stands. West of Shotley Bridge. At the Gatehouse on Burnmill Bank, just West of Shotley Hall turn-off. Toll house built in the 19th century by the Lobley Hill Turnpike Trust. Operating until after 1830.
BlacksterXOn the turnpike from Shotley Bridge to Edmondbyers (1815-1873). East of the Derwent bridge. At the junction just West of the Eddysbridge turn-off. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating in 1830.
AxwellXOn the A694, the turnpike from new Swalwell Bridge through Rowlands Gill to Shotley Bridge (1833-1888). Street Gate, near the entrance to Damhead Car Park and Swalwell Cricket Club (Wayside Cottage). Toll house built in the 19th century. Operation until after 1830.
Rowlands GillXOn the A694, the newer turnpike through Rowlands Gill to Shotley Bridge (1833-1888). At junction between Station Road and Burnopfield Road Toll house built in the 19th century. Operation until after 1830.
DerwentcoteXOn the A694, the newer turnpike from Swalwell through Rowlands Gill to Shotley Bridge (1833-1888). Toll house still stands. Derwentcote Cottage; West of the turn-off for Medomsley and Burnopfield; adjacent to Byerside Wood. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating until after 1830.
Shotley BridgeXOn the newer Gateshead to Shotley Bridge turnpike road through Litzford and Hamsterley (1833-1888), now the A694, loosely following the line of the Derwent. Street Gate, near East Law; about midway between Shotley Bridge and Ebchester. Toll house built in the 19th century. Operating until after 1830.
Wylam BridgeXThe new toll house for Wylam Bridge was built in 1899 by a bridge trust on the North side of bridge and still stands. The original toll house built in 1836 by a bridge trust for the collection of tolls on the south side of the bridge was demolished in 1960.
Scotswood BridgeXOn the A695, the Scotswood Bridge Toll. Earliest evidence: 1833). North side of Scotswood Bridge (the old Chain Bridge) just East of the modern Bridge. Toll house built in 1831. Operating until after 1830.
ElswickXClaimed to be a toll point for the Scotswood Road approch to an unnammed bridge. Earliest evidence: 1838. However, it's not close to any particular bridge in Newcastle and even further from Scotswood Bridge. Further investigation is required here.
Old Redheugh BridgeXToll bar for old Redheugh Bridge, just West of the modern bridge. Now an unclassified road. Toll house still stands. Old toll house; at the south end of Redheugh Bridge. Alongside the A608. Toll house built in 1871 by a bridge trust. Operating until after 1830.
Swing BridgeXBridge Street: junction with Sandhill and Fish Quay; North end of the High Level Bridge Toll house built in 1876.
High Level BridgeXOn the North side of the Bridge, between the Castle Keep and the Bridge Hotel. Tolls, initially of one penny, were charged until abolition of tolls in 1937. Bridge designed by Robert Stephenson and opened on 15 August 1849.
Ovingham BridgeXAt the eastern end of the Bywell turnpike, linking Ovingham village with the road to Prudhoe. Brooklands Road; still a ford on the 6” OS map; right on the North side of the bridge Toll house built in 1883 by a bridge trust. Operating until after 1830.
function hideIt(x) { var t = document.getElementById(x); t.style.display = "none"; }
function showIt(x) { var t = document.getElementById(x); t.style.display = ""; }
function toggle(x,y) { var t = document.getElementById(x) if (t.style.display == "none") {showIt(x); showIt(y);} else {hideIt(x); hideIt(y);} }
function hiLight(x, c1, c2) {
t = document.getElementById(x);
if(t.style.fill == c1) {t.style.fill = c2;} else {t.style.fill = c1;}
}
function switchIt(x) { var t = document.getElementById(x) if (t.style.display == "none") {showIt(x);} else {hideIt(x);} } // function greenFill() {
var items =['Ph1_btn','Ph2_btn','Ph3_btn','Ph4_btn','Ph5_btn','Brg_btn']; var j = document.getElementById('allOnOff_btn'); var flag =0
for ( var count =0; count < items.length; count++) { var p =items[count] if (document.getElementById(p).style.fill=='green') { flag++; } if(flag==items.length) {j.style.fill='green';} }
} //
function redFill(x) { var t = document.getElementById(x) t.style.fill='red'; }
// function allOnOff(...items) { //this creates a function with unlimited parameters assigned to an array called items[]
var flag =0 // create a flag to be incremented if any element of the array is not hidden for ( var count =0; count < items.length; count++) { //loop through the elements of the array var x =items[count] if (document.getElementById(x).style.display!='none') { // if the element is visible increment the flag flag++; } } //after the loop has finished if all elements are visible the flag will have the same value as the array length for ( var count =0; count < items.length; count++) { // loop through the elements of the array again var x =items[count]
if (flag==items.length) { // if all the elements of the array are visible hide the element otherwise show it hideIt(x); } else { showIt(x); } } } //
// function changeFills() {
var items =['Ph1_btn','Ph2_btn','Ph3_btn','Ph4_btn','Ph5_btn','Brg_btn']; var t =document.getElementById('allOnOff_btn'); if(t.style.fill=='green'){ for(var count =0; count<items.length;count++){ var x=items[count] var p=document.getElementById(x) p.style.fill="green"; } } else{ for(var count =0; count<items.length;count++){ var x=items[count] var p=document.getElementById(x) p.style.fill="red"; } } } //
/* The following script has been adapted from a tutorial by Peter Collingridge - see https://www.petercollingridge.co.uk/tutorials In addition to the script is the code to create a controller for panning and zooming and style sheets called by the controller module.
In order to zoom and pan the map independently the layers to be panned and zoomed should be grouped and named 'matrix-group'- this is best done by Inkscape. Using a text editor search for 'matrix-group' to locate it. Edit the entry to include the following after id=matrix-group: transform="matrix(1 0 0 1 0 0)"
Go to the start of the code and change the id in the svg tag to id=”map-svg”
function pointer is used in the controller code - it changes the cursor to a pointer on a mouseover*/
var transformMatrix = [1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0]; var svg = document.getElementById('map-svg'); var viewbox = svg.getAttributeNS(null, "viewBox").split(" "); var centerX = parseFloat(viewbox[2]) / 2; var centerY = parseFloat(viewbox[3]) / 2; var matrixGroup = svg.getElementById("matrix-group"); var flag=0
function pan(dx, dy) {
if (flag==1){transformMatrix=[1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0];} transformMatrix[4] += dx; transformMatrix[5] += dy; flag=0; setMatrix(); } function zoom(scale) { if (flag==1){transformMatrix=[1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0];} for (var i = 0; i < 6; i++) { transformMatrix[i] *= scale; } transformMatrix[4] += (1 - scale) * centerX; transformMatrix[5] += (1 - scale) * centerY; flag=0 setMatrix(); } function setMatrix() { var newMatrix = "matrix(" + transformMatrix.join(' ') + ")"; matrixGroup.setAttributeNS(null, "transform", newMatrix); }
function reset() {
flag=1 var matrixGroup = svg.getElementById("matrix-group"); matrixGroup.setAttributeNS(null, "transform", transformMatrix);
}
function pointer(x) { document.getElementById(x).style.cursor = "pointer"; }
function arrow(x) { document.getElementById(x).style.cursor = "default"; }
function makeDraggable(evt) { var svg = evt.target; svg.addEventListener('mousedown', startDrag); svg.addEventListener('mousemove', drag); svg.addEventListener('mouseup', endDrag); svg.addEventListener('mouseleave', endDrag); svg.addEventListener('touchstart', startDrag); svg.addEventListener('touchmove', drag); svg.addEventListener('touchend', endDrag); svg.addEventListener('touchleave', endDrag); svg.addEventListener('touchcancel', endDrag); function getMousePosition(evt) { var CTM = svg.getScreenCTM(); if (evt.touches) { evt = evt.touches[0]; } return { x: (evt.clientX - CTM.e) / CTM.a, y: (evt.clientY - CTM.f) / CTM.d }; } var selectedElement, offset, transform; function initialiseDragging(evt) { offset = getMousePosition(evt); // Make sure the first transform on the element is a translate transform var transforms = selectedElement.transform.baseVal; if (transforms.length === 0 || transforms.getItem(0).type !== SVGTransform.SVG_TRANSFORM_TRANSLATE) { // Create an transform that translates by (0, 0) var translate = svg.createSVGTransform(); translate.setTranslate(0, 0); selectedElement.transform.baseVal.insertItemBefore(translate, 0); } // Get initial translation transform = transforms.getItem(0); offset.x -= transform.matrix.e; offset.y -= transform.matrix.f; } function startDrag(evt) { if (evt.target.classList.contains('draggable')) { selectedElement = evt.target; initialiseDragging(evt); } else if (evt.target.parentNode.classList.contains('draggable-group')) { selectedElement = evt.target.parentNode; initialiseDragging(evt); } } function drag(evt) { if (selectedElement) { evt.preventDefault(); var coord = getMousePosition(evt); transform.setTranslate(coord.x - offset.x, coord.y - offset.y); } } function endDrag(evt) { selectedElement = false; } }
